fast.summary: This paper proposes a system to automatically recommend product search queries that appear alongside Q&A widgets (like 'People Also Ask') on e-commerce sites, so relevant products can be displayed under each answer. The approach uses customer co-click data to train a model that generates queries meeting defined quality criteria, outperforming baselines in both offline and online evaluations.
fast.key_idea: Leverage customer co-click behavior on e-commerce platforms as a supervision signal to train a query recommendation model that bridges natural language Q&A content and product catalog search.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners building e-commerce search or recommendation systems can use this approach to increase customer engagement by seamlessly connecting informational content (Q&As) to transactional intent (product discovery), going beyond traditional query rewriting or expansion techniques.
possible_use_cases: Enriching 'Customers Also Ask' widgets on e-commerce platforms with contextually relevant product carousels
Improving product discoverability for users in the exploratory/research phase of shopping
Generating high-quality product search queries from FAQ or support content to power recommendation engines

deep.method_breakdown: The system generates product query recommendations to display relevant products beneath Q&A widgets (e.g., 'Customers Also Ask Alexa') on e-commerce sites. A model is trained using customer co-click data — when users click on a Q&A and then click on a product query — to learn associations between questions and high-quality product search queries. The model generates queries satisfying defined tenets: relevance, specificity, diversity, and shoppability, evaluated both offline and via online A/B testing.
deep.key_innovation: Unlike query rewriting or expansion (which reformulate an existing query) or keyphrase extraction (which pulls terms from text), this work frames query recommendation as a generation task conditioned on a Q&A pair, guided by co-click behavioral signals rather than lexical similarity. The use of proprietary customer co-click data to define 'good' recommendations — rather than annotator judgments or query logs alone — is the distinguishing data-side contribution.
deep.technical_insight: The critical design choice is using co-click supervision: queries that real customers clicked after seeing a Q&A serve as training targets, ensuring generated queries are both semantically related to the question and actually shoppable. This behavioral grounding bypasses the gap between linguistic relevance and commercial utility that purely text-based methods suffer from.
deep.limitations: The approach depends heavily on proprietary co-click logs, making it non-transferable to cold-start Q&As or low-traffic pages with sparse behavioral data. The tenets (relevance, shoppability, diversity) are partially operationalized through proxy metrics, so the offline evaluation may not fully capture real-world quality. The system is evaluated on a single e-commerce platform, limiting generalizability to other domains or search engines.
interview_questions: How did you define and operationalize the tenets for high-quality query recommendations, and how did you verify that your offline metrics aligned with online engagement?
Why is co-click data a better supervision signal than annotator-labeled relevance judgments for this task?
How does your method handle Q&As with little or no co-click history — i.e., the cold-start problem?
What distinguishes query recommendation from query rewriting and keyphrase generation in terms of both the input/output formulation and the training objective?
How did you measure 'diversity' among generated queries, and what tradeoffs did you observe between diversity and relevance in your results?
interview_answers: The tenets — relevance, specificity, shoppability, and diversity — are defined to capture what makes a product query useful in a Q&A context. Offline proxies include BLEU/ROUGE against co-clicked queries and shoppability classifiers; alignment with online CTR and engagement rates was validated through A/B testing, confirming that offline gains translated to real customer engagement improvements.

Annotator labels capture surface-level semantic relevance but miss whether a query actually leads to satisfying product results. Co-click data embeds real customer intent: if users clicked a query after reading a Q&A, that query is both relevant and actionable in the product catalog, providing a richer and more commercially grounded training signal.

Cold-start Q&As lack co-click supervision, so the model must rely on generalizable patterns learned from seen Q&A types. The paper acknowledges this as a limitation; potential mitigations include content-based fallbacks or transferring signals from similar Q&As, but this is not fully addressed in the current work.

Query rewriting takes an existing query and paraphrases or expands it, staying close to the original formulation. Keyphrase generation extracts salient terms from a document. Query recommendation here takes a question-answer pair as input and generates a novel product search query, requiring understanding of both the informational intent of the Q&A and the commercial query space — a fundamentally different conditioning and generation objective.

Diversity is measured by lexical and semantic dissimilarity among the top-k generated queries for a given Q&A. The tradeoff observed is that maximizing relevance tends to collapse queries around the most obvious product category, while diversity-promoting objectives (e.g., sampling strategies, reranking) can introduce queries that are slightly less precise but cover a broader product space, ultimately improving coverage and engagement across user segments.
